exemplar.title:
How does different household incomes of individuals in Klang influence their
awareness of photovoltaic systems (PV) in reducing air pollution?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does different household incomes of individuals in Klang influence their
awareness of photovoltaic systems (PV) in reducing air pollution?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Follows IB structure with logical progression from data to interpretation
Uses appropriate ESS and statistical terminology accurately
weaknesses: Large raw tables and formatting errors disrupt readability and flow
Report exceeds concision guidelines and contains some repetition
Figure captions and formatting inconsistencies impede smooth comprehension
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and logic are generally maintained (Band 2), and terminology is used correctly but not concisely (Band 2). Occasional lapses in coherence and presentation keep performance in the mid-band, meriting 2 marks.

strengths: Formulates a precise, focused research question identifying income categories, PV awareness and geographic scope
Provides strong environmental context linking PMââ exceedances in Klang to health and visibility concerns
weaknesses: Includes peripheral global mortality data reducing local focus
Asserts socioeconomic influences on PV awareness without fully unpacking causal mechanisms and confounding factors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The RQ is coherent, focused and consistently applied (Band 6). The environmental issue is well contextualized, though some data are peripheral (Band 5). Connections between income, education and PV awareness are described but with minor omissions (Band 4). Overall performance falls solidly in the 5â6 range but with some gaps, so a holistic mark of 5 accurately reflects strong but not fully comprehensive coverage.

strengths: Designs a repeatable Google-form survey with clear variables and planned statistical tests
Justifies quota sampling effectively to represent all income brackets
weaknesses: Omits details on coding non-responses, handling missing data and ANOVA assumptions
Ethical section lacks full treatment of data storage, withdrawal rights and bias mitigation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The method is repeatable and generally suited to the research aim (Band 4), and the sampling strategy is fully justified (Band 5). Ethical considerations are outlined but not comprehensive (Band 3). These strand performances average to a mid-band level, warranting 4 marks.

strengths: Evaluates reliability using error bars and p-values, acknowledging missing factors
Identifies clear strengths (reach, cost) and weaknesses (social-desirability bias) of the method
Suggests meaningful mixed-methods improvements and further research
weaknesses: Does not evaluate broader environmental implications of the conclusion
Improvement suggestions are not explicitly linked to each weakness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation of the conclusion, method and improvements is present but contains omissions in environmental context, sampling-bias discussion and linkage of fixes to weaknesses. This corresponds to mid-band performance (Band 3) across all strands, yielding 3 marks.

strengths: Presents all relevant data in appropriate tables and charts
Analyzes data correctly and completely, identifying clear group patterns
Draws conclusions fully supported by statistical evidence (P < 0.05)
weaknesses: Minor formatting glitches in tables and figure captions
Typographical error in ANOVA degrees of freedom header
Needs clarification on choice of population vs. sample SD formula
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation, analysis and conclusion fully meet the top-band descriptors: all relevant data are visualized, patterns are interpreted accurately, and conclusions are validly supported. Minor formatting and a header error do not detract from meeting the 5â6 criteria, justifying a top score of 6.

strengths: Proposes a government programme tightly aligned with findings on B40 awareness
Justifies the application based on data-driven economic rationale
weaknesses: Evaluation of solution omits scalability and unintended environmental impacts
Does not fully explore trade-offs in the proposed application
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application proposal meets the top descriptor (Band 3), but solution evaluation is only partial (Band 2). Overall performance aligns with mid-band for Applications, justifying 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key ESS and statistical terms are used accurately (PM10, ANOVA, standard deviation), but the report exceeds concision guidelines and contains typographical clutter, meeting the âeitherâbut not bothâ descriptor.

The student comments on reliability (error bars, p-value) and acknowledges missing factors, but does not examine broader environmental implications or alternative interpretations in depth; thus evaluation is present yet contains omissions.

The conclusion restates trends and ties them directly to statistical significance (P<0.05), clearly linked to the hypothesis.  Evidence from figures and ANOVA is cited, satisfying the requirement for a fully-supported, valid conclusion.

Means, standard deviations and a one-way ANOVA are calculated correctly and interpreted; patterns between income groups are explicitly identified.  While a df error appears in the table header, the analytic logic is intact and covers all data sets.

A meaningful improvement (mixed-methods) and extension variables are suggested, yet they remain broad and are not explicitly linked to rectifying each stated weakness; therefore they meet mid-band expectations.

A repeatable Google-form survey with fixed variables, scoring rubric and planned statistics is outlined.  Yet specifics on question coding, treatment of non-responses and ANOVA assumptions are absent, so the design, while serviceable, may not yield âsufficientâ depth for all objectives.

The connection between income, education access and awareness of PV is described and related literature cited.  However, the mechanism is asserted rather than fully unpacked, and potential confounding factors are only mentioned later, so minor omissions remain.

The student formulates a precise, answerable question that identifies all key variables (household-income categories, awareness, geographic focus on Klang and photovoltaic systems).  No ambiguity or double-barrel phrasing is present, and the wording remains consistent throughout the report, satisfying the descriptor for a coherent and focused RQ.

Strengths (awareness, poverty alleviation) and weaknesses (cost, access) are weighed, but aspects such as scalability and unintended environmental impacts are omitted, so only partial evaluation is achieved.
The IA follows the IB section headings and flows logically, yet large blocks of raw tables disrupt readability, so structure is present but not sustained flawlessly.

Strengths (large reach, low cost) and weaknesses (social-desirability bias, closed questions) of the survey are discussed, but limitations such as sampling bias or Likert scaling issues are not fully developed.  Coverage is descriptive rather than exhaustive.

The introduction explains particulate pollution, Malaysian fossil-fuel dependence and Klangâs historic PM10 exceedances, linking these clearly to health and visibility.  The depth is good, but some data (e.g. Harvard global deaths) is peripheral, so the treatment is strong but not fully sophisticated.

Quota sampling is not only described but justified (representation of the three income brackets, citation provided).  Although non-random limitations are admitted later, the rationale for meeting the research aim is explicit and adequate, matching the top-band requirement.

Ethical measures (informed consent, confidentiality) are stated and the study poses negligible physical risk, but no reference is made to data storage, withdrawal rights or potential bias mitigation, so coverage is outline-level rather than comprehensive.

All relevant raw data are summarised in frequency tables and multiple clearly-labelled charts (bar, pie, error-bar).  Minor formatting glitches exist, yet every variable central to the enquiry is visualised appropriately, fulfilling the descriptor for complete presentation.
A targeted government programme (MySuria-style) is clearly linked to the finding of low B40 awareness, and economic justification is provided.  The alignment with data achieves the top descriptor.

Arguments generally progress logically from data to interpretation; however, formatting errors and occasional repetition impede smooth reading, so coherence is achieved mainly, but with difficult sections.

annotations.comment: The research question is precise and focused, identifying key variables (income categories, PV awareness) and the geographic scope (Klang) clearly, facilitating targeted investigation.
The introduction provides strong environmental context but incorporates peripheral global mortality data; consider focusing on local PM10 exceedances for deeper relevance.
The connection section asserts socioeconomic influences on PV awareness but omits detailed mechanisms; unpack causal pathways and potential confounders for completeness.
The variables table outlines independent, dependent, and fixed factors well but lacks detail on coding non-responses and handling missing data; specify these to ensure method repeatability.
Quota sampling is clearly justified to ensure representation across income brackets, demonstrating sound sampling strategy aligned with research aims.
Ethical considerations omit data storage protocols and withdrawal rights; include these to comprehensively address participant protection and data security.
Tables present all demographic and awareness data clearly with percentages; the comprehensive presentation meets full-data visualization standards.
The mean formula is correctly displayed but would benefit from a brief reminder of symbol definitions inline to aid reader comprehension.
The standard deviation calculation uses population formula; clarify if nâ1 should be used for sample data or justify population approach.
The ANOVA table reports df=4 for between groups but with three income groups df should be 2; correct this to ensure statistical accuracy.
The conclusion restates statistical findings (p<0.05) and ties results to the hypothesis clearly, providing a valid evidence-supported interpretation.
Evaluation of the conclusion mentions error bars but omits broader environmental implications; include interpretation in context of air-quality improvements.
The discussion identifies strengths and weaknesses of the survey but neglects samplingâbias limitations; address representativeness concerns in the evaluation.
Consider adding qualitative interviews or focus groups to deepen insights on why income influences PV awareness, enhancing the studyâs scope and depth.
The policy proposal focuses appropriately on the B40 bracket and aligns with findings; the targeted government MySuria scheme justification is well supported.
Solution evaluation mentions accessibility and cost but omits scalability and unintended environmental impacts; expand evaluation of potential trade-offs.
The RQ title is repeated in the hypothesis section; streamline to avoid redundancy and improve conciseness.
Survey question formatting is inconsistent (Likert anchors and numbering); standardize scale presentation to enhance clarity and reduce respondent error.
Large raw tables disrupt readability; consider summarizing key results and moving detailed data to an appendix to improve report flow.
Figure 3.0 and 4.0 axes labels could be more descriptive (e.g., include units or full question text) and error bars explained in captions for complete communication.
The attitude questions table is fully comprehensive, presenting means and standard deviations for each group, facilitating thorough comparison.
Challenge data table is clear and presents both counts and percentages; consider aligning rows for better readability given multi-row question text.
The individual score matrix is overly dense; split into summary statistics and present raw scores in an appendix to maintain coherence.
Figure 9.0 illustrates group means and SDs effectively; add a note on p-value threshold and df to reinforce the ANOVA interpretation.
